extensionDescription.message: Tor integration and anti-fingerprinting for Thunderbird — TorBirdy successor.

scrubLoginsLabel.message: Delete saved passwords for onion/loopback mail servers when disabling

disableHelp.message: Disable restores the prefs snapshot taken before hardening was first enabled. Clearnet accounts that were intentionally bypassed keep working normally.

messageIdIntro.message: The FQDN in outgoing Message-IDs is a recurring fingerprint across your mail. Default uses your From-address domain so you blend with regular users at the same provider. TorBirdy's localhost.localdomain mode remains available as an explicit legacy choice.

torTestIntro.message: Checks whether a local Tor SOCKS endpoint is reachable without sending mail or changing Thunderbird prefs. The probe uses Tor's SOCKS5 RESOLVE command with a fresh random isolation token, so the target host is not resolved through system DNS.

torTestOk.message: Tor SOCKS is reachable at $1:$2 ($3). No Thunderbird prefs were changed.

torTestNoTor.message: No reachable Tor SOCKS endpoint found. Tried: $1

dnsLeakIntro.message: Compares the IP that Tor returns via SOCKS5 RESOLVE with the IP returned by your system resolver. A divergence may mean the OS is using non-Tor DNS; CDN-backed names can still be benign.

canaryVerdictLeak.message: system resolver answer is not in any Tor circuit's view; verify system DNS routing

canaryVerdictOk.message: system_ip is in the Tor set ($1 circuit(s) tried)

canaryVerdictDiverged.message: system_ip is not in the Tor set, but the heuristic says this is probably benign; inspect details

canaryVerdictMissingData.message: one of the lookups did not return data

canaryErrorTor9050.message: Tor is not answering on 127.0.0.1:9050. Start the Tor daemon, or use port 9150 for Tor Browser.

canaryErrorTor9150.message: Tor Browser is not running. Start it; its SOCKS listener is usually on 9150.

canaryErrorSocksRefused.message: SOCKS connection refused. Check the configured Tor host and port.

canaryErrorNetworkUnreachable.message: The network blocks outbound traffic on this path. Bridges or pluggable transports may be needed.

canaryErrorTimeout.message: Tor is reachable, but the circuit did not finish in time. Try again or rotate circuits.

canaryErrorDnsStatus.message: The OS resolver returned an error. For full protection, system DNS should use a Tor DNSPort or run inside Whonix/Tails.

disableConfirm.message: This restores your prefs to the snapshot taken before hardening was first enabled. Tor-only sends may go back over clearnet. Continue?

fqdnInvalid.message: custom FQDN must be a valid multi-label DNS name, not an IP address

helpTldrDoes1.message: Routes Thunderbird IMAP and SMTP through a local Tor SOCKS proxy and keeps DNS resolution remote through Tor.

helpTldrDoes2.message: Fails closed: if Tor is unreachable, mail sending should fail instead of falling back to clearnet.

helpTldrDoes3.message: Reduces mail fingerprints such as User-Agent, X-Mailer, HELO, timezone, Message-ID FQDN, flowed text and remote content.

helpTldrDoes4.message: Offers a Tor availability test and a DNS canary; both use Tor's SOCKS5 RESOLVE path and avoid system DNS for the Tor side.

helpTldrDoesNot1.message: It does not replace Tails, Whonix, a Tor gateway, or a correctly configured system resolver.

helpTldrDoesNot2.message: It does not hide the mailbox you authenticate with from your provider or from Authentication-Results headers added by the provider.

helpTldrDoesNot3.message: It does not hide the fact that a recipient may see a Tor exit in Received headers.

helpTldrDoesNot4.message: It does not protect other apps, OS-level leaks, compromised endpoints, or network-correlation attacks.

helpTldrTorbirdy1.message: TorBirdy was a legacy Thunderbird/XUL extension. Thunderbird 78 removed the legacy XUL overlay extension system it depended on.

helpTldrTorbirdy2.message: Its last release was in 2018, so it cannot run on modern Thunderbird ESR builds without a rewrite.

helpTldrExperimentsTitle.message: Why the Experiments API is needed

helpTldrExperiments1.message: Normal MailExtensions cannot change Thunderbird's proxy prefs, SMTP server internals, identity prefs, DNS cache, login manager, or raw SOCKS self-tests.

helpTldrExperiments2.message: The Experiments API exposes those Thunderbird internals; OnionBird gates writes through an allowlist to keep the privileged surface small.

helpNerdDoes1.message: Proxy prefs: network.proxy.type=1, SOCKS5 host/port auto-probe, socks_remote_dns=true, failover_direct=false and no proxy bypass list.

helpNerdDoes2.message: Header hygiene: suppresses User-Agent/X-Mailer, forces HELO/EHLO to [127.0.0.1], picks a safer Message-ID FQDN, disables flowed plaintext and pushes UTC/RFP-related behavior.

helpNerdDoes3.message: Network hardening: disables DoH/TRR, OCSP, speculative connects, DNS prefetch, predictor, WebRTC, geolocation, ECH HTTPS-RR lookup, captive-portal checks and update phone-home URLs.

helpNerdDoes4.message: Mail UI hardening: disables remote mail content paths, plaintext rendering, notification leaks, address-book auto-collect and saved Tor-login audit/removal on disable.

helpNerdDoes5.message: Monitoring: startup and periodic reassert repair hardened prefs; test mode checks Tor SOCKS without pref writes, and a stream-isolated SOCKS5 RESOLVE canary checks system DNS behavior.

helpNerdDoesNot1.message: Authenticated SMTP still lets the provider know the account, and providers may add Authentication-Results smtp.auth values visible to recipients.

helpNerdDoesNot2.message: SMTP transport still creates Received chains. The recipient can often infer Tor usage from the provider's Tor-exit-facing hop.

helpNerdDoesNot3.message: The add-on controls Thunderbird, not the operating system. NTP, clipboard, files, other applications, crash dumps outside TB and hostnames remain OS-threat-model issues.

helpNerdDoesNot4.message: The canary is detection, not magic routing. On stock desktops, route OS DNS through Tor DNSPort or use Whonix/Tails for the strongest guarantee.

helpNerdDoesNot5.message: It does not defeat global passive adversaries, end-to-end timing correlation, malicious providers, malicious recipients, or compromised Thunderbird/profile state.

helpNerdTorbirdy1.message: TorBirdy targeted the old privileged XUL/XPCOM add-on model and used overlay/bootstrap hooks into Thunderbird internals.

helpNerdTorbirdy2.message: Thunderbird 78 removed legacy XUL extensions; modern Thunderbird accepts WebExtensions/MailExtensions, which deliberately do not expose those privileged mail and pref internals.

helpNerdTorbirdy3.message: Porting TorBirdy is therefore not a manifest bump. The privileged pieces must be rewritten behind a Thunderbird Experiments API.

helpNerdExperiments1.message: The MailExtension side owns UI, runtime messages and options; the Experiments parent script owns Services.prefs, MailServices SMTP/identity objects, DNS cache flushing, login-manager audit and raw SOCKS5 RESOLVE.

helpNerdExperiments2.message: This split is necessary because public MailExtension APIs cannot atomically snapshot/restore prefs or set per-SMTP HELO/TLS settings.

helpNerdExperiments3.message: Because Experiments run with parent-process privilege, OnionBird restricts pref writes to a narrow allowlist and serializes hardening mutations.

sendBlockedCancelStorageError.message: OnionBird: outgoing send blocked — addon state cannot be read (storage error). Restart Thunderbird, then check OnionBird Options before retrying.

sendBlockedCancelLeakDetected.message: OnionBird: outgoing send blocked — Tor leak canary detected a DNS resolution that did not traverse Tor. Open OnionBird Options → DNS leak status → Run self-test now before retrying.

sendBlockedCancelNoVerdict.message: OnionBird: outgoing send blocked — no canary verdict yet (canary has not run or storage was cleared). Open OnionBird Options → DNS leak status → Run self-test now before retrying.

sendBlockedCancelOther.message: OnionBird: outgoing send blocked — canary verdict is not 'clean' (state: $STATE$). Open OnionBird Options → DNS leak status → Run self-test now before retrying.

socksOverrideIntro.message: By default the addon detects Thunderbird's existing network.proxy.socks pref or falls back to 127.0.0.1 on port 9050 / 9150. Set host + port here to pin a different endpoint (e.g. a Whonix Gateway IP). Host must be a loopback name (localhost) or an IP literal — DNS-resolvable names would leak via the system resolver before Tor.

socksOverrideStatusInvalidHost.message: Invalid host — must be `localhost` or an IP literal (IPv4 or IPv6).

socksOverrideStatusInvalidPort.message: Invalid port — must be an integer between 1 and 65535.

socksOverrideTbPrefWarning.message: Thunderbird currently has $TB$ configured; your saved override $OVERRIDE$ will replace it on next enable.

socksOverrideStatusReset.message: Override cleared — falling back to auto-detect on next enable.

autoEnableNotificationMessageSocksUnreac.message: OnionBird could not reach a Tor SOCKS endpoint on 127.0.0.1:9050 or :9150. Outgoing sends are blocked until Tor is reachable. Start the Tor daemon (or Tor Browser) and open OnionBird Options → DNS leak status → Run self-test now.

autoEnableNotificationMessageCanaryFail.message: OnionBird auto-enabled but the canary self-test did not verify cleanly. Outgoing sends are blocked until the next canary passes. Open OnionBird Options → DNS leak status → Run self-test now to triage.
